summary: Riccomini explores why there's no true cloud-hosted Prometheus, discovering that its single-node storage design is intentional rather than a limitation. This architecture prioritizes simplicity for getting started while offering flexibility through remote storage integrations when scaling becomes necessary. He then pivots to searching for an embedded time-series database purpose-built for metrics workloads, ultimately discovering FrostDB from PolarSignals. FrostDB uses an LSM tree structure with Apache Arrow and Parquet, designed specifically for high-write, immutable observability data with dynamic schemas.
key_points: Prometheus deliberately uses single-node storage to simplify initial setup, deferring the complexity of distributed systems until users actually need to scale
The lack of true cloud-hosted Prometheus is by design—users can choose from dozens of remote storage backends when scaling out
Metrics workloads have distinct characteristics (high writes, low reads, immutable data, wide/dynamic schemas) that general OLAP databases don't optimize for
FrostDB is an embeddable columnar database using an LSM tree structure with Arrow for hot data and Parquet for cold storage
Parca leverages FrostDB to provide continuous CPU profiling through a single eBPF filter
money_quotes: I was surprised to find that there isn't any Prometheus cloud hosting.
Prometheus is intentionally designed with only single-node storage.
Getting started is easy because you don't have to set up a distributed system. Users can defer the scaling decision until later.
This architecture is all about user choice. Kudos to the Prometheus designers.
OLAP DBs should work, but I was really looking for something purpose-built for common metric characteristics.
It's built exactly as I'd hoped—a log-structured merge-tree (LSM). The first level stores Apache Arrow records. Data is then rewritten into Parquet for subsequent levels.
key_insight: Prometheus's single-node architecture is a deliberate design choice that prioritizes simplicity and user choice over built-in distributed storage, enabling an ecosystem of remote backends rather than locking users into one scaling path.
